captions.text: Maybe it's because I'm past the age that I have  to start counting my grey hairs in square inches,
but the central pitch of playing a James Bond  video game has lost its effect somewhat. "Have
you ever imagined BEING JAMES BOND?  Jet setting to glamorous international
locations and very deliberately avoiding eye  contact with poor people?" Yes, and it all
seems terribly exhausting. I watch the end of  a typical James Bond film and I think, "Wait,
you're gonna shag a hot lady right now? You  just beat up all the bad guys and escaped from
an exploding doom fortress; I'd have thought  you'd be ready for a sleep." If it were me,
I'd be too preoccupied with thoughts of filing  my collateral damage report to be in the mood
for a muff dive right now. But then I've  never been shortlisted for the role. 007:
First Light is the origin story of MI6 superspy  James Bond. "Which one?" you might ask. Well,
it's set in a contemporary time period, so none of  the established ones. They don't put an exact year
on it, but they keep mentioning "HIS Majesty's  Secret Service," so it's gotta be recent;
I assume they're not talking about George VI.  Or... I dunno. Aslan. The game comes to us from IO
Interactive, best known for the Hitman series, and  isn't this a pairing written in the fucking stars?
Hm, who would be best suited to make a game about  infiltrating various high-class locales as an
omnicompetent, secretive, violent bastard with  a codename ending in 7 and who pulls off a suit
really well? Hm. You got a moment, IO Interactive?  If you can take time away from fending off demands
for Kane & Lynch 3. Of course, James Bond and  Agent 47 have very different personalities,
meaning that James Bond has one, so  there are some necessary differences in,
for want of a better word, execution. Gameplay  wise, First Light goes back and forth between
Hitman-style open-ended levels full of stealth  challenge and social engineering opportunities
and Uncharted-style linear action set pieces.  Which altogether is much more Bondy. Much more
getting a suntan on Bondi Beach, if you will.  Because where Agent 47 is always in control,
James Bond is not. He starts out looking cool  infiltrating a high-class drinks do, slyly
giving random women orgasms with his remote joy  buzzer cufflinks, but sooner or later, the format
demands he's going to have to fuck up in a way  that means he has to drive a stolen Lamborghini
through Michelangelo's statue of David and then  bludgeon someone to death with a stone penis.
And I have to admit, I was won over in the end.  My concern at the prospect of playing an origin
story is that you could make an action game  about literally any fuckface with a gun and
call it a James Bond game, but before he was  James Bond, but this was unwarranted. First
Light hits all the right notes. The dude looks  like a young amalgam of every James Bond actor:
he's got Connery's eyebrows, Dalton's nose, Roger  Moore's slightly weak jawline. There's gunfights;
punch-ups; fast cars; hot, mysterious birds and  the shagging thereof; and of course, gadgets.
MI6 apparently being perfectly happy giving their  expensive experimental tech to trainees. 'Cause
of course the British government's just had money  to burn since Brexit caused that massive economic
boom. Ironic tone of voice. The plot opens with  young James Bond—or James Bond Junior, if you
will—as a lowly aircrewman who single-handedly  destroys a criminal operation after the rest of
his team are killed, after which MI6 calls him  in and goes, "We want you to work directly for
the government because you refused to listen to  professional advice and destroyed a whole bunch of
shit, and that's the kind of attitude that could  make you Prime Minister someday." From there,
we go through a somewhat unprecedented  concept: a playable training montage.
Cutting rapidly between the punching tutorial,  the shooting tutorial, and the doing press-ups
tutorial, which will come in handy when the  shagging of hot ladies comes into things. After
that, Bond starts going on missions, and from  there, the plot becomes a smidge predictable.
The no-nonsense mentor does the mentoring, the  mysterious love interest does mysterious things,
and, well, if you don't immediately identify  who the evil mastermind turns out to be the
very first time they appear on screen, then  I don't know what to tell you, frankly,
except to take more comprehension tests.  But if James Bond wanted to surprise us,
he'd go to Alcoholics Anonymous and start  forming meaningful relationships with people,
none of which would be terribly interesting to  watch. So, as you were, James Bond, you fucking
nutcase. So, as I say, about half the missions  are Hitman-style open-ended levels where the
prime strategy is using your gadget phone that's  about one step removed from a magic wand to futz
with random electronics, distracting guards long  enough for you to grab the thing they're guarding,
give them the old anesthetic sucker punch, or  gain access to the ridiculously large ventilation
shafts apparently designed to simultaneously  circulate air and transport cattle between rooms.
But in contrast to Agent 47, Bond actually has  charisma, which means if he gets caught behind
enemy lines, he has options other than scowlingly  hurling a steam iron at someone's face. Hence the
Bluff mechanic, wherein you can press a button  and Bond confidently rattles off an excuse
for why he's here and why he's pulling down an  unconscious bellhop's trousers, and the nearby
enemies will buy it for just long enough for you  to confidently speed-walk the fuck to the next
room. Very authentic and quite satisfying to pull  off, although in practical terms, it's essentially
an extra life system for when you fuck up the  stealth. And you need a certain amount of stamina
or energy or mojo or whatever it is to pull it  off, a resource most easily restored by knocking
people out, which doesn't make a whole lot of  sense in context—that you have to desperately
struggle-snuggle four guys to help you remember  how to be a nice, civilized person—but never mind.
The Hitman-style gameplay tends to be easier and  with fewer options than straight Hitman classic,
but that's what you should expect when it's woven  into a larger tapestry of gameplay that also has
to involve beating the shit out of people and  driving cars into things that aren't garages.
Thus, linear action set pieces. But the standard  combat's got a psychotic improvisational flair
to it that I found rather appealing. Bond goes  absolutely Daniel Craig on motherfuckers. You
run out of bullets, throw your empty gun at the  closest guy, charge in, and just go bugfuck:
shoving; kicking; smashing heads into walls and  desks; randomly grabbing expensive ornaments off
shelves and pounding them into faces; generally  attacking with the frenzied desperation of someone
who knows that four other guys in the room are  already aiming their guns and will draw a bead the
instant you stop to breathe or shake the teeth off  your lapels. So, to get to some kind of summary:
stealth's good; action's good; I think we did it,  boys—it's a James Bond game. With a plot that's
enjoyable if not mind blowing and which goes on  a touch too long. Three fucking times it feels
like it's ending and then keeps going. Thwart  bad guy; save world. Whoops, not quite done;
new chapter. Thwart OTHER bad guy; save world  again. Oh, shit, we didn't sufficiently thwart
the first bad guy; thwart him more thoroughly;  save world; maybe this time remember to put a
fucking fence around the world or something.  Man, how much thwarting do you expect me
to do today? I need to save some thwarting  energy for when I go to thoftball practith.
